en.extract:
The Tumbesian tyrannulet or Tumbes tyrannulet (Nesotriccus tumbezanus) is a species of bird in subfamily Elaeniinae of family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Ecuador and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
What is now the Tumbesian tyrannulet was previously considered a subspecies of what was then the mouse-colored tyrannulet. The unsplit species bore the binomial Phaeomyias murina. Genetic analysis showed that Phaeomyias was embedded within Nesotriccus and by the principle of priority, beginning in 2018 most taxonomists moved the species to Nesotriccus. However, as of late 2024 BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World (HBW) retained the species in genus Phaeomyias.
Beyond the move to genus Nesotriccus the Tumbesian tyrannulet's taxonomy is unsettled. Based on multiple lines of evidence the mouse-colored tyrannulet was split into several species, one of which is the Tumbesian tyrannulet. The International Ornithological Committee (IOC) and the Clements taxonomy assign it two subspecies, the nominate N. t. tumbezanus (Taczanowski, 1877) and N. t. inflavus (Chapman, 1924). HBW includes a third subspecies, maranonica, which the IOC and Clements treat as a separate species, the Maranon tyrannulet (N. maranonicus).
The Tumbesian tyrannulet's English name comes from the Department of Tumbes, Peru, which is approximately in the center of its range.
This article follows the two-subspecies model.


Description
The Tumbesian tyrannulet is about 12 cm (4.7 in) long. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a drab gray-brown head with an indistinct whitish supercilium. Their upperparts and tail are drab brownish gray. Their wings are dusky with somewhat ochraceous or buff tips on the coverts that show as two wing bars. Their throat, breast, and flanks are grayish and their belly pale yellow. Subspecies N. t. inflavus has a paler gray breast and flanks than the nominate and a whitish belly. Both sexes of both subspecies have a brown iris, a stubby bill with cream at the base of the mandible, and black legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The Tumbesian tyrannulet is a bird of the coastal lowlands to foothills of Ecuador and Peru. The nominate subspecies is found from central Manabí Province in west-central Ecuador south into northwestern Peru through eastern Tumbes and Piura departments into northeastern Lambayeque Department. Subspecies N. t. inflavus is found in Peru from central Piura and Lambayeque departments south to northern Lima Department. The species primarily inhabits arid lowland scrublands and open woodlands, but especially in southern Ecuador inhabits moister valleys penetrating the Andean foothills to the east. In elevation it ranges from sea level to 2,300 m (7,500 ft) in Ecuador and to 2,000 m (6,600 ft) in Peru.


Behavior


Movement
The Tumbesian tyrannulet apparently is partially migratory though details are lacking. For instance, it is known from the Cerro Blanco Forest in Ecuador only between July and November.


Feeding
As far as is known, the Tumbesian tyrannulet's diet and foraging behavior are the same as those of the southern mouse-colored tyrannulet, which see here.


Breeding
The Tumbesian tyrannulet breeds between early February and mid-April. Its nest is a cup made from leaf veins, seed down, small twigs, and feathers. It is typically placed in a branch fork of a bush or small tree up to about 5 m (15 ft) above the ground. The usual clutch size is two or three eggs. The incubation period is 16 to 17 days and fledging usually occurs 14 to 15 days after hatch. Details of parental care are not known.


Vocalization
The Tumbesian tyrannulet's dawn song is "a grating, harsh dzzree-dzzree'DJEE". Its calls are "various explosive, sneezy, buzzy sounds: DZEEK! or dzz-CHEW! and a harsh growl".


Status
The IUCN follows HBW taxonomy and so has not separately assessed the Tumbesian and Maranon tyrannulets. The combined taxon is assessed as of Least Concern with no immediate threats identified. The Tumbesian tyrannulet is known from several protected areas in both countries. It "might presumably benefit from some degree of deforestation and it is unlikely to be threatened in the future".


References

The '''Tumbesian tyrannulet''' or '''Tumbes tyrannulet''' ('''''Nesotriccus tumbezanus''''') is a species of bird in subfamily Elaeniinae of family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Ecuador and Peru.<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

What is now the Tumbesian tyrannulet was previously considered a subspecies of what was then the mouse-colored tyrannulet. The unsplit species bore the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] ''Phaeomyias murina''. Genetic analysis showed that ''Phaeomyias'' was embedded within ''Nesotriccus'' and by the [[principle of priority]], beginning in 2018 most taxonomists moved the species to ''Nesotriccus''.<ref name=59Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Kevin J. Burns, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., Douglas F. Stotz, Benjamin M. Winger, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-ninth supplement to the American Ornithological Society s ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2018, vol. 135:798-813 retrieved December 13, 2022</ref> However, as of late 2024 [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' (HBW) retained the species in genus ''Phaeomyias''.<ref name=HBW8.1>HBW and BirdLife International (2024). Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 8.1. Available at: https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/taxonomy retrieved August 26, 2024</ref>

Beyond the move to genus ''Nesotriccus'' the Tumbesian tyrannulet's taxonomy is unsettled. Based on multiple lines of evidence the mouse-colored tyrannulet was split into several species, one of which is the Tumbesian tyrannulet.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Rheindt | first1=Frank E. | last2=Norman | first2=Janette A. | last3=Christidis | first3=Les | title=Genetic differentiation across the Andes in two pan-Neotropical tyrant-flycatcher species | journal=Emu | year=2008 | volume=108 | issue=3 | pages=261–268 |doi=10.1071/mu08020| bibcode=2008EmuAO.108..261R }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Zucker, M.R.|author2= Harvey, M.G.|author3= Oswald, J.A.|author4= Cuervo, A.|author5= Derryberry, E. |name-list-style=and |author6=Brumfield, R.T. |year=2016|title=The Mouse-colored Tyrannulet (''Phaeomyias murina'') is a species complex that includes the Cocos Flycatcher (''Nesotriccus ridgwayi''), an island form that underwent a population bottleneck|journal= Mol. Phylogenet. Evol. |volume=101|pages= 294–302|doi= 10.1016/j.ympev.2016.04.031|pmid= 27126184|bibcode= 2016MolPE.101..294Z}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Harvey, M. G.|author2= G. A. Bravo|author3= S. Claramunt|author4= A. M. Cuervo|author5= G. E. Derryberry|author6= J. Battilana|author7= G. F. Seeholzer|author8= J. S. McKay|author9= B. C. O'Meara|author10= B. C. Faircloth|author11= S. V. Edwards |author12=J. Pérez-Emán|author13= R. G. Moyle|author14= F. H. Sheldon|author15= A. Aleixo|author16= B. T. Smith|author17= R. T. Chesser|author18= L. F. Silveira|author19= J. Cracraft|author20= R. T. Brumfield |author21= E. P. Derryberry |year=2020|title=The evolution of a tropical biodiversity hotspot|journal= Science |volume=370|issue= 6522|pages=1343–1348|doi= 10.1126/science.aaz6970|pmid= 33303617|bibcode= 2020Sci...370.1343H|hdl= 10138/329703|hdl-access= free}}</ref> The [[International Ornithological Committee]] (IOC) and the [[Clements taxonomy]] assign it two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''N. t. tumbezanus'' ([[Władysław Taczanowski|Taczanowski]], 1877) and ''N. t. inflavus'' ([[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1924). HBW includes a third subspecies, ''maranonica'', which the IOC and Clements treat as a separate species, the [[Maranon tyrannulet]] (''N. maranonicus'').<ref name=IOC14.2/><ref name=Clements2023>Clements, J. F., P.C. Rasmussen, T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, A. Spencer, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2023. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2023. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved October 28, 2023</ref><ref name=HBW8.1>HBW and BirdLife International (2024). Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 8.1. Available at: https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/taxonomy retrieved August 26, 2024</ref>

The Tumbesian tyrannulet's English name comes from the [[Department of Tumbes]], Peru, which is approximately in the center of its range.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop987.htm |title=Establish English names for ''Nesotriccus murinus'' complex |last=Remsen |first=J.V. Jr. |date=December 2023 |website=South American Classification Committee |access-date=October 17, 2024 }}</ref>

This article follows the two-subspecies model.

==Description==

The Tumbesian tyrannulet is about {{convert|12|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a drab gray-brown head with an indistinct whitish [[supercilium]]. Their upperparts and tail are drab brownish gray. Their wings are dusky with somewhat ochraceous or buff tips on the [[coverts]] that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their throat, breast, and flanks are grayish and their belly pale yellow. Subspecies ''N. t. inflavus'' has a paler gray breast and flanks than the nominate and a whitish belly. Both sexes of both subspecies have a brown iris, a stubby bill with cream at the base of the [[mandible]], and black legs and feet.<ref name=TUTY-BOW>{{cite taxon|BOW|author=Fitzpatrick, J. W.|author2= J. del Hoyo|author3= G. M. Kirwan|author4= N. Collar |year=2023|title= Tumbes Tyrannulet (''Nesotriccus tumbezanus'')|version= 1.1 |editor=B. K. Keeney|doi=10.2173/bow.moctyr6.01.1 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/moctyr6/1.1/introduction |access-date= October 17, 2024}}</ref><ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last1 =Ridgely | first1 =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | page =460 | isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref><ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last1 =Schulenberg | first1 =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | page =416  |isbn = 978-0-691-13023-1 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Tumbesian tyrannulet is a bird of the coastal lowlands to foothills of Ecuador and Peru. The nominate subspecies is found from central [[Manabí Province]] in west-central Ecuador south into northwestern Peru through eastern [[Department of Tumbes|Tumbes]] and [[Department of Piura|Piura]] departments into northeastern [[Lambayeque Department]]. Subspecies ''N. t. inflavus'' is found in Peru from central Piura and Lambayeque departments south to northern [[Lima Department]]. The species primarily inhabits arid lowland scrublands and open woodlands, but especially in southern Ecuador inhabits moister valleys penetrating the Andean foothills to the east. In elevation it ranges from sea level to {{cvt|2300|m|ft}} in Ecuador and to {{cvt|2000|m|ft}} in Peru.<ref name=TUTY-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Tumbesian tyrannulet apparently is partially migratory though details are lacking. For instance, it is known from the [[Cerro Blanco Forest]] in Ecuador only between July and November.<ref name=TUTY-BOW/>

===Feeding===

As far as is known, the Tumbesian tyrannulet's diet and foraging behavior are the same as those of the southern mouse-colored tyrannulet, which see [[Southern mouse-colored tyrannulet#Feeding|here]].<ref name=TUTY-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Tumbesian tyrannulet breeds between early February and mid-April. Its nest is a cup made from leaf veins, seed down, small twigs, and feathers. It is typically placed in a branch fork of a bush or small tree up to about {{convert|5|m|ft|round=5|abbr=on}} above the ground. The usual clutch size is two or three eggs. The incubation period is 16 to 17 days and fledging usually occurs 14 to 15 days after hatch. Details of parental care are not known.<ref name=TUTY-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Phaeomyias-tumbezana |species=the Tumbesian tyrannulet}}
===Vocalization===

The Tumbesian tyrannulet's dawn song is "a grating, harsh ''dzzree-dzzree'DJEE''". Its calls are "various explosive, sneezy, buzzy sounds: ''DZEEK!'' or ''dzz-CHEW!'' and a harsh growl".<ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] follows HBW taxonomy and so has not separately assessed the Tumbesian and Maranon tyrannulets. The combined taxon is assessed as of Least Concern with no immediate threats identified.<ref name=IUCN/> The Tumbesian tyrannulet is known from several protected areas in both countries. It "might presumably benefit from some degree of deforestation and it is unlikely to be threatened in the future".<ref name=TUTY-BOW/>
